sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident escalation** – no documented path for complaints (class quality, billing, conduct, injury), investigation, resolution, or when to involve leadership/legal
- **Financial reconciliation & discrepancies** – no SOP for daily/weekly cash/card reconciliation, refund authorization, or chargeback handling
- **Coach performance management & improvement plans** – no formal process for tracking attendance, member feedback, underperformance flags, or remedial coaching
- **Health & safety incident reporting** – no documented protocol for falls, injuries, equipment failure, near-misses, accident book entry, or insurance notification
- **Member win-back/churn prevention** – no systematic process for identifying at-risk members, outreach cadence, or re-engagement offers
- **Staff scheduling conflicts & callout procedures** – no SOP for managing sickness, emergency cover, or last-minute class cancellations
- **Onboarding post-sign-up** – no documented member experience from payment through first class (email sequence, studio arrival, induction, buddy assignment)
- **Data privacy & GDPR compliance** – no process for member data requests, deletion, consent management, or breach reporting
- **Supplier/vendor management** – no SOP for equipment maintenance contracts, ordering, payment terms, or issue escalation

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coach SOPs (17/17)** – likely missing: attendance expectations, lateness policy, social media conduct, how to handle member complaints directed at them, safeguarding/welfare concerns, and progression criteria
- **Client SOPs (22/22)** – review for gaps: membership pause/freeze process, billing dispute handling, class cancellation/refund policy, and member conduct/banning thresholds
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)** – Email Newsletter & Blog Post exist as gaps;

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTION PLAN

## REVENUE GROWTH (Priority 1: Fix the leakage)

• **Churn is killing momentum.** June hit 0% attrition (excellent), but May was 5.74% and Mar–Apr averaged 7.5%. You added 25 net members Sep–Dec, then lost 30 in Jan–Apr despite the bigger studio. Target: hold churn below 2% consistently. Audit why Jan–Mar saw 7.45%, 2.88%, 6.60%—likely post-Christmas drop-off and relocation disruption. Implement exit interviews and a "at-risk" retention call programme now.

• **AR/M (revenue per active member) has collapsed.** June was £120.48; September–December averaged £151–170. You're bringing members through the door but charging less or losing premium upsells. Audit: are class packages/PT sessions being offered? Test a price increase of 5–7% on new sign-ups (from approx £200/month standard rate implied by 85 × £162.33 = £13.8k) and bundle ancillaries (nutrition coaching, merchandise, guest passes). Target: £160+ AR/M by August.

• **December spiked to £21.1k (49.5% margin)—why?** Reverse-engineer: seasonal gift memberships, New Year resolutions, or one-off event revenue. Replicate in June–July (summer body season) with a June campaign: introduce discounted 3-month blocks (paid upfront) and corporate team memberships. Your 147 active members in June is your highest base; don't leave that on the table.

---

## EXPENSE CONTROL (Biggest red flag: non-people costs)

• **Non-people expenses surged Jan–Apr (avg £11.1k/month vs Sep–Dec avg £4.9k) and remain elevated in May–June (£9.2–9.4k).** This is 50–60% of revenue in recent months vs 33–35% in Q4. Excluding April's relocation, you're still running £8–9k/month. Drill into line items: rent (new studio larger?), utilities, insurance, supplies, software. Negotiate with landlord for a fixed rent cap; audit utilities (LED lighting, smart thermostats); challenge vendor contracts monthly. **Target: reduce to £6.5k/month within 90 days (32% of revenue).**

• **People costs are stable at £3.1–3.7k/month (17–20% of revenue).

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately:** Her Build: Lower (17 sessions), Build: Upper (9), Elevate (6) and Strongevity (3) total 35 class slots. Assign Build: Lower to Eilis (she needs lower-intensity reps to build confidence before tackling Tuesdays) and split Build: Upper + Elevate + Strongevity between JoJo and Eilis based on existing strengths. This removes Grace from delivery.

• **Move Grace's 9 Body Composition Scans to admin or rotating duty:** These aren't real coaching—any staff member can conduct them. Free up ~3 hours of Grace's time and stop treating them as coaching load across the team.

• **Rebalance JoJo's PT load:** At 44 PTs last month, JoJo is carrying almost all 1:1 work. Target 25–30 PTs for JoJo (keeping her as lead), assign 8–12 to Eilis (in smaller batches to build her client book and confidence), and reassign any of Grace's PT clients proportionally. This prevents burnout and develops Eilis.

• **Assign Eilis "Build: Lower" as her signature class:** Give her 2–3 recurring slots per week. It's lower-skill than Build: Upper or Tuesdays, builds her coaching reputation, and fills the gap Grace leaves without overloading her before Tuesdays readiness.

• **Audit class demand vs. coverage:** If any class (e.g. Circuits, Mobility, Plate) is light or runs sporadically, consolidate or shift to Eilis to give her more floor time. Avoid creating new single-coach dependencies—every class should have a backup coach trained and ready.

• **Cap Grace's admin at 3 hours/week

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio gaps between PT and classes:**

1. Launch a "Open Studio" slot on Thursday and Monday evenings (your quietest days) where members can book 30-min paid PT blocks or small-group sessions (2-3 people) at a lower rate than full PT — this captures demand without needing a scheduled class and uses dead time.

2. Run a monthly Saturday morning "Strength Workshop" (45 mins, ticketed £8-12) focusing on one movement pattern each month — deadlift, squat, bench — targeting Build class members who might want technique depth; Saturday is quiet so this adds revenue without competing with busy days.

**For the quieter classes themselves:**

3. Move **Engine** and **Capacity** to Wednesday or Tuesday (your peak days) to ride higher foot traffic, and offer first-timers a free trial week on these two classes specifically — position them as "conditioning foundations" for Build class graduates in promotional posts on class booking or in-gym signage.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — The Forge

**Cut the guessing game fast:**
- **Do this today:** List every service you currently offer (gym access tiers, PT sessions, classes, nutrition coaching, etc.). Assign each a price and what's bundled. Don't overthink—write what's real now, not what "should" exist. This becomes your baseline.

**Make it coach-facing first:**
- **Internal menu for staff:** Create a one-pager (literally one sheet) showing coaches *exactly* what they can sell, at what price point, and what they're responsible for delivering. Include commission/cut so they're incentivized to upsell correctly. Without this, coaches either undersell or promise things you can't deliver.

**Design for member clarity (conversion):**
- **External menu logic:** Structure by *outcome, not service type*. Instead of "Small Group PT – £45/session," try "8-week strength foundation – £320" or "Unlimited gym + 2 PT sessions/month – £89." Price anchoring matters—put premium packages visibly to make mid-tier packages feel like the "smart choice."

**Test it immediately:**
- **Run this on 5 current members this week:** Show them your menu, ask which package they'd buy if starting fresh, where they're confused. One round of feedback kills assumptions. Update based on what they actually respond to.

**Lock it into systems:**
- **Integrate into booking/payment NOW:** Get it live in your app/system before coaches start pitching alternatives. Friction in implementation = pricing chaos within a month.

What's your highest-priced offering currently, and what's it bundled with?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Execution for The Forge

**1. Mine your actual customer conversations NOW**
- Pull the last 50 enquiry emails, Instagram DMs, and trial sign-up forms. What questions do they ask? ("Do you have childcare?" "What if I'm not fit enough?" "Is it judgment-free?") These ARE your landing page headlines and FAQs — not guesses.
- Record 3-5 existing members answering "Why did you choose The Forge?" — use their exact language as social proof on the page.

**2. Create a "clarity ladder" specific to your positioning**
- Above the fold: One sentence that stops scrolling. For a women's-only gym, this might be "Strength training without the ego" or "Built BY women, FOR women" — test which one your ideal member responds to.
- Then answer in order: *What is it? Who's it for? What will I actually DO? How much? How do I start?* — each as its own section, scannable in 10 seconds.

**3. Ruthlessly test "common objections" as page sections**
- Add a dedicated section: "Starting from zero?" / "Worried about judgment?" / "Unsure if it's for you?" — answer these directly with member testimonials or before/after context, not gym-speak.
- Embed a 60-second walkthrough video of a real session. Removes mystery faster than any copy.

**4. A/B test your CTA by friction level**
- Test "Book Free Trial" vs. "Quick Question? Message Us" vs. "See Our Schedule" — different messaging attracts different confidence levels. Track which converts by member quality,

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Execute Member Feedback System at The Forge

• **Start stupidly small this week**: Create a 4-question Google Form (takes 20 mins). Post the link in your WhatsApp group, email blast, and print it at reception. Don't overthink it—"What class do you want?", "What's not working?", "Rate your experience 1-10", "Refer a friend?" Done. You'll get 15-20 responses and actual data within 7 days.

• **Make it a post-class ritual, not an admin task**: Assign ONE staff member to hand out printed post-it feedback cards during peak hours (say, Thu-Sat evenings). Takes 30 seconds per member. You'll capture feedback when emotions are fresh and get higher participation than a form link ever will.

• **Turn one piece of feedback into visible action monthly**: Pick the single most-mentioned complaint or request (e.g., "need more morning classes" or "change shower schedule"). Announce the fix in your next member email with *why* you made it. This proves you're listening and dramatically increases future feedback submissions.

• **Use this as a sales tool, not just improvement**: When touring prospects, casually mention "our members literally shaped our schedule"—it's differentiation. You'll convert better because women want to be heard, not sold to.

• **Set a hard deadline**: Results review + action plan on the 25th of each month. 15-minute team huddle. Otherwise the feedback pile grows and nothing changes, killing trust.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Reverse-engineer from complaints & bottlenecks first**
Before writing anything, spend 2 hours interviewing your staff asking: "What question do members ask repeatedly? Where do people get confused?" This prevents writing SOPs nobody needs. Your actual problem areas (fuel forward ordering delays? well75 booking chaos?) get priority—write those SOPs first, not in alphabetical order.

**2. Use a dead-simple template & assign ONE person per SOP**
Don't try to write all five yourself. Create a one-page template (member journey → decision points → exact steps → who to contact). Assign each SOP to the staff member who *actually does that job daily*—your PT coordinator writes the PT SOP, not you. They know the real friction points. You review & edit for clarity only.

**3. Test SOPs with a real member before finalizing**
Hand the draft SOP to an actual new member in that category and watch them follow it without help. Where they hesitate = where your SOP is broken. Revise, then it goes live. This takes 1 hour per SOP but saves weeks of misunderstanding.

**4. Make them *scannable*, not readable**
Format as: "Your first session: ① Book here [link] ② Arrive 15 mins early ③ Meet [name] at desk." Bullet points. One page max. Members won't read paragraphs. If it's longer than one page, you've overcomplicated it.

**5. Build a living SOP tracker**
Create a simple spreadsheet: SOP name | owner | last updated | next review date. Review quarterly and update based on

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar — Execute This Week

• **Start with a 4-week template, not a year's worth.** Use a simple Google Sheet with columns: Date | Platform | Content Type | Hook | Asset Status. Fill only 4 weeks at a time—this prevents overwhelm and lets you adjust based on what actually drives engagement at The Forge. Review metrics Friday afternoon, tweak Monday's plan.

• **Batch-create content in a single 90-minute session bi-weekly.** Film 8 short videos (transformations, quick workouts, member spotlights, behind-the-scenes) on Day 1. Shoot photos on Day 2. This beats daily scrambling and gives you a visual consistency your audience will recognize.

• **Assign content pillars tied to *why* women join you:** Strength Progress (40% of posts—transformation clips, PRs), Community/Belonging (35%—member stories, group energy), Education (25%—form tips, nutrition). This forces relevance and stops random posting.

• **Use scheduling tool TODAY** (Buffer, Later, or Meta Business Suite—all free-tier sufficient). This removes the friction of remembering to post and lets you batch uploads. Schedule the full 4 weeks every other Friday morning in 20 minutes.

• **Make one member your "content champion" this month.** Offer free month or merchandise in exchange for letting you film short clips of her journey 2x/week. Authentic member stories outperform any branded content and build tribal loyalty.

What's your biggest blocker right now—creating content ideas, or the time to actually film?

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Videography Sessions: Execution Plan for Grace

• **Lock in the first 3 sessions NOW** — Don't book "regularly" vaguely. Call Jordan today, pick fixed dates (e.g., every 8 weeks on a Tuesday morning), add to both calendars. This removes the friction that kills follow-through.

• **Assign a content calendar to yourself** — Before each session, decide what 3-4 specific moments/outcomes you want captured (new class launch, member transformation story, equipment tour). Brief Jordan 48 hours prior. This turns raw footage into usable assets instead of hours of unusable video.

• **Repurpose aggressively from day one** — One 2-hour shoot = 8-10 reels/TikToks, 2-3 Instagram posts, 1 testimonial video, email content. Map where each piece goes *before* filming. Otherwise footage sits unused.

• **Tie it to revenue** — Track which video type drives inquiries/sign-ups (usually testimonials and transformations beat facility tours). Double down on what converts. After 3 sessions, you'll have data to justify the spend.

• **Make Jordan your go-to, not a vendor** — Build a relationship. Give him early access to new class formats or member stories. Familiarity = faster turnaround, better creative instincts, and he'll prioritize your bookings.

What's your biggest bottleneck right now — getting the actual filming done, or turning footage into content?

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Automating Trial Enquiry Follow-ups for The Forge

• **Set up conditional email sequences in Mailchimp (free tier works):** Create ONE core sequence triggered on "trial enquiry submitted" → Day 1 (welcome + gym tour booking link), Day 3 (social proof from current members), Day 6 (limited-time offer to convert). Skip generic reminders; each email has ONE job.

• **Use Calendly + Zapier ($20/month) to auto-book gym tours:** When someone books a trial, Zapier automatically adds them to a shared calendar view AND sends Grace a text alert. Kills the "someone forgets to follow up" problem that tanks conversions.

• **Build a simple tracking checklist in Airtable (free):** Each enquiry shows: name → email sent → tour booked → attended → converted. Takes 90 seconds to build. Grace glances at it weekly to spot drop-off points (if 60% never book the tour, that's your real problem, not email).

• **Test ONE friction point this week:** Go back 10 trial enquiries from last month. Where did they disappear? Was it the initial response time? No clear tour booking option? Fix that one thing, not everything. Re-run the system for 2 weeks, measure conversions.

• **Automate the "show up" reminder:** SMS (Twilio) 24 hours before booked tour with a "reply YES to confirm" option. Cuts no-shows by 30-40%. If they don't confirm, auto-trigger a same-day call from Grace or a staff member.

**What's the actual current drop-off rate from enquiry →

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist + KPI Documentation for The Forge

**1. Role-specific anchor metrics (not generic ones)**
- **JoJo (likely ops/classes):** New member attendance conversion rate (%) + class utilization rate (%) + retention month-on-month
- **Eilis (likely sales/growth):** Trial-to-paid conversion + cost-per-acquisition + member referral rate
- **Sarah (likely community/retention):** Active engagement rate (check-ins/month) + churn rate + upsell revenue per member
- Map 2-3 metrics MAX per person so they're not drowning

**2. Build the checklist backwards from KPI targets**
- Write what each person *must do weekly* to move their KPI (not random tasks)
- Example for JoJo: "Analyze last week's attendance data Tuesday AM → identify low-performing class times → adjust schedule by Wednesday" (directly impacts utilization KPI)
- This forces accountability: they can't claim they "tried" if the action is specific

**3. Format it as a simple shared doc with weekly columns**
- Google Sheet: role name | 3-4 weekly actions | owner KPI | status (green/amber/red) | actual result
- Review together every Monday morning (10 mins max) — talk only about red/amber items
- This becomes your weekly standup agenda, not a separate meeting

**4. Gate it with one monthly review**
- After 4 weeks, pull the data. Did their actions move the KPI? If not, the action list is wrong — not them
- Adjust the checklist in week 5 based on what actually moved the needle

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session Execution for The Forge

**Before the session (do this NOW):**
- **Write a 1-pager on SSO + 90-day plan** — not a PowerPoint. One page forces clarity. Include: what success looks like in 90 days (e.g., "250 active members," "£X revenue"), the 3-4 key moves to get there, and why it matters to *them personally* (pay rises, quieter chaos, portfolio-building).

**During the session (2 hours max):**
- **Start with personal asks** — go around the room: "What's one thing you want from The Forge in the next 90 days?" (development, flexibility, recognition). Write them down. Then show how the plan makes *that* possible. Trust builds here.
- **Assign ownership, not tasks** — don't say "Janet handles marketing." Say "Janet: you own member acquisition funnels. You decide the *how*. Here's the budget/tools. Success = X new signups by Day 90." Give her decision-making power.
- **Create visible accountability** — on a whiteboard in the gym, map each person's 90-day lane (3 measurable outcomes each). Leave it up. Weekly 10-min huddles reviewing it. She sees her impact.

**Right after:**
- **Send a 5-sentence recap email** — their name, their lane, the 1-2 metrics they own, and one specific decision they now have autonomy over. Make it feel different from "another meeting."

Grace needs her team *running toward something*, not following orders. This session is where that happens.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit + Rebalance for The Forge (30 Days)

**Week 1 — Map actual vs. perceived work:**
- Have each team member (instructors, admin, PT staff) log their hours by task category for 5 days using a simple spreadsheet — you'll spot if someone's doing unpaid admin while another runs one class daily. Don't ask "are you busy?" — the data doesn't lie.

**Week 2 — Find the bottlenecks:**
- Pull your revenue data and match it to who owns each income stream (class bookings, PT sales, member retention calls, billing issues). You'll likely find one person is the single point of failure for something critical. That's your overstretch zone.

**Week 3 — Create the swap:**
- Identify 2-3 specific tasks the stretched person can hand off (not vague delegation — concrete tasks: "Friday admin reports go to X," "new member check-ins go to Y"). Cross-train one backup for each so you're never dependent on one person again.

**Week 4 — Implement + anchor it:**
- Roll out the new structure, but also block time in *your* calendar to do one task from the rebalanced person's old list for 2 weeks. You'll see friction points they didn't mention and prove it's actually doable differently.

**The real win:** By day 30, you should see reduced sick days and turnover, plus one team member visibly more available for revenue-generating work (PT sales, member experience, retention).

What's the biggest bottleneck you already suspect?

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge (10% Target)

**Audit your fixed costs ruthlessly:**
- Pull your last 3 months of invoices. You're likely overpaying on something — utilities, insurance, or cleaning contracts. Call vendors and ask them directly: "What's your best rate for annual commitment?" Most will drop 5-15% without negotiation resistance. Start with your top 3 expense lines.

**Renegotiate supplier terms, not just price:**
- Don't just ask for discounts — shift to quarterly payments instead of monthly (better for their cash flow = leverage for you). Or bulk-buy cleaning supplies/equipment maintenance at 20% savings. This feels less painful than rate cuts and suppliers prefer it.

**Cut the membership "leakage" destroying margins:**
- How many inactive members are still paying? Identify anyone who hasn't checked in 60+ days and either win them back (one personal outreach call = often 50% re-activation) or let them go. Dead weight kills profitability silently. Target: 8-12% member churn reduction = instant margin gain.

**Optimize staffing without cutting hours members see:**
- Consolidate your lowest-attendance class times. If your 6am Tuesday slot has 3 people, merge it with another class. Redeploy that trainer to peak hours or 1-on-1 premium sessions (higher margin). Keep what sells, cut what doesn't.

**Quick win — review your payment processing fees:**
- You're bleeding 2-3% on card fees. Switch to a loyalty/app-based payment system or negotiate tiered rates with your processor. Small shift, real money.

**Target: Hit

# Executing: Update Monthly Finance KPIs for The Forge

• **Pull actuals NOW, not end-of-month** — Don't wait for clean data. Export bank statements + membership software today (even if messy) and map: MRR by membership tier, churn rate, class utilization %, cost per acquisition (ad spend ÷ new members). Raw numbers beat perfect delays.

• **Flag the 2-3 metrics that actually drive decisions** — Grace doesn't need 15 KPIs. Identify: What makes/loses money fastest? (Likely: monthly recurring revenue, churn %, and class capacity %). Build a one-page dashboard comparing this month vs. last month vs. target. Everything else is noise.

• **Spot the action signal immediately** — When you update, don't just log it. Ask: *Is churn up? Cancel a marketing channel.* *MRR down? Push 3-month memberships this week.* *Underutilized class? Kill it and reallocate that trainer.* Update = decision trigger, not admin task.

• **Automate this so it takes 15 minutes next month** — If this took 2 hours, you're doing it manually. Set up Zapier/Make to pull membership data weekly into a Google Sheet template. Grace should spend time *interpreting*, not data-gathering.

• **Share with your team Friday AM** — Post the KPIs (and what they mean) in a Slack channel. One sentence per metric. Creates accountability and catches problems early.

What's the biggest friction point in pulling your numbers right now?

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Build a 24-month cash-flow model in a spreadsheet TODAY** — not a fancy system. Columns: membership revenue (segment by tier), class pack sales, PT sessions, operating costs (rent, staff, utilities), seasonal dips (January peak, summer slump). Start with last 3 months of actual data to validate assumptions. This becomes your decision-making backbone.

• **Reverse-engineer your break-even number** — Calculate: fixed monthly costs ÷ average member LTV (lifetime value). If it's 80 members at £40/month, you know exactly what growth target matters. Grace can then pressure-test: "Can we realistically hit 100 members in 6 months?" If yes, forecast shows profit. If no, something needs cutting or pricing needs shifting.

• **Run 3 scenarios: conservative, realistic, stretch** — Not to predict the future, but to expose risks. Conservative: 5% monthly churn, realistic: 2%, stretch: 0.5%. Show Grace what her decision about staff hiring *actually costs* under each scenario. This stops her making commitments based on hope.

• **Update monthly, 30 minutes only** — Actual revenue in, adjust assumptions, compare vs forecast. The forecast only matters if it's maintained. Grace spots revenue pattern breakdowns or cost overruns within weeks, not quarterly surprises.

• **Use forecasts to negotiate** — When talking to landlord about expansion or staff about raises, forecasts show what's affordable. Data kills emotional arguments.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Build Your Service P&L — The Forge Edition

**Get messy with actual numbers first (today):**
- Pull last 3 months of invoices/POS data and categorize every pound by service type. Don't aim for perfection—assign classes to "group fitness," PT sessions to "1:1 coaching," protein shakes to "add-ons." This takes 90 minutes max and beats waiting for a "perfect system."

**Calculate true unit economics (not just revenue):**
- For *classes*: Divide total monthly class instructor costs by total class attendances to find cost-per-spot. Then multiply average class size × membership fee allocated to classes. The gap = your margin per class offered. Classes likely look better than they are because fixed costs hide low attendance classes dragging profit.
- For *PT*: Revenue is straightforward; subtract PT trainer cost (hourly rate × actual hours booked) + any equipment/facility allocation. PT probably crushes classes on margins but moves fewer units—this matters for cash flow.
- For *add-ons*: Track what women actually buy (protein, merch, supplements). Cost is easy; attach realistic retail markup—don't use manufacturer suggestions.

**Spot your cash trap immediately:**
- Look for low-attendance classes running on fixed instructor schedules. These are profit killers that feel "free" because they're grouped with memberships. Cut or consolidate the bottom 2 by next week.

**Test one pricing move based on findings:**
- If add-ons margin is 40%+, reposition them hard at checkout (POS display, trainer recommendations). If PT margin vastly exceeds classes, run one promoted PT package offer next month to shift mix.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit Execution for The Forge Automations

• **Run a parallel test first** — pick 3-5 dummy email addresses and submit them through your enquiry form today. Track exactly what they receive and when. This reveals duplicates immediately (e.g., same welcome email sent twice) and dead-ends in your flow before you touch anything live.

• **Map your actual conversion funnel on paper** — list every automation that fires between enquiry submission and first trial class attendance (welcome emails, payment reminders, class booking confirmations, etc.). Duplicates usually live here. Use your Zapier/Automations dashboard to visually connect the triggers and actions—you'll spot redundant steps fast.

• **Check your payment gateway integration** — if trial bookings flow through Stripe or similar, confirm the "trial booked" signal actually triggers your follow-up sequence. Broken integrations here are the #1 reason enquiries go dark. Test one live transaction yourself if needed.

• **Audit unsubscribe/bounce data** — log into your email platform (likely Mailchimp, ActiveCampaign, etc.) and filter for bounces in the last 30 days. High bounces = broken email fields in your form. Fix the data capture, not just the automation.

• **Create a simple decision tree document** — once you've removed duplicates, document the exact sequence: Enquiry → Welcome Email (Day 0) → Trial Booking Link (Day 1) → Pre-Class Reminder (Day before). Share it with whoever manages memberships. This is your audit record and prevents future chaos.

**Timeline: 4-6 hours max. Do this this week.**

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge – Concrete Execution

**Task 1: Member Check-in & Attendance Reminders**
- Use Zapier to connect your gym management software (Mindbody, Zen Planner, etc.) → WhatsApp/SMS via Twilio. Set it to auto-send 24-hour class reminders to members who've booked but have a cancellation history. This recaptures 8-12% of no-shows with zero manual effort. Cost: ~£20/month.

**Task 2: Email Member Onboarding Sequence**
- Build a 5-email drip campaign in Mailchimp triggered by "new member signup." Each email guides them through induction schedule → gym etiquette → class booking → referral incentive. Grace reviews and personalizes once, then it runs forever. You'll need 2-3 hours upfront. Result: Higher retention + referrals baked into Day 1.

**Task 3: Instagram Caption & Story Generation**
- Use ChatGPT (or Jasper) with a simple prompt template: "Write an energetic 150-char Instagram caption about [class type], include [location hashtag], end with [call-to-action]." Store templates in a Google Doc. Record voice notes of your best captions, feed them to the AI as a style guide. Saves 30 mins/week. Post consistency = algorithm boost.

**Start here:** Pick Task 1 (highest ROI), spend 1 hour mapping your current system, then configure the Zapier automation. You'll see impact in 2 weeks.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cyber Security for The Forge Website — Execution Plan

• **Get an SSL certificate TODAY** — non-negotiable. Use Let's Encrypt (free) or Cloudflare (£15/month). If your site shows "not secure," potential members bounce immediately and Google ranks you lower. This takes 30 mins max.

• **Audit payment processing NOW** — if you take card details (memberships, classes), you need PCI compliance. Use Stripe or Square (they handle most compliance for you). Don't store card data yourself. Check your current setup in the next 48 hours.

• **Password-protect your admin areas properly** — change default login URLs, use strong passwords (12+ characters), enable two-factor authentication on WordPress/Shopify/whatever platform you use. Most breaches happen here, not through fancy hacking.

• **Backup your site weekly to a separate location** — use Updraft Plus (WordPress) or your hosting's built-in backup tool. If you get ransomwared, you recover in hours instead of losing everything. Set it and forget it (literally automate it).

• **Get basic liability coverage clarity** — contact your business insurance provider TODAY and confirm you're covered if customer data gets compromised. One cyber incident can tank a fitness business; make sure you're protected legally.

**Why this matters for Grace:** Members trust you with payment info AND personal fitness goals. A breach = lost members + legal headaches. This isn't optional.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

1. **You've fallen off a cliff.** July's £27 CPL is 4.5x April's £6, and you're getting 17x fewer leads despite spending only 75% less. This isn't efficiency loss—it's campaign collapse. The ad account or creative is likely broken, not optimised.

2. **Close rate is actually your bright spot.** July hit 75% (3 of 4 sales), which is elite for fitness trial lead-gen. April was 29%, May was 61%, June was 65%. Something in your nurture or sales process improved—hold that tight.

3. **The spend cliff started in May.** CPL doubled May-to-June even as you cut spend by £105. This suggests audience saturation, creative fatigue, or the algorithm struggling with shrinking budget. July's tiny spend (£109) probably triggered Meta's learning phase without enough daily budget to complete it.

**Actions**

1. **Rebuild budget to £300–350/month minimum immediately.** You need £10–15/day to keep Meta's algorithm in stable delivery. At £109, you're undershooting the threshold. Allocate this across 2–3 fresh audience segments (lookalikes of your best closers, women 25–45 interested in fitness + wellness, retargeting website visitors from past 180 days).

2. **Audit and refresh creative NOW.** You're likely hitting ad fatigue hard. Pull last month's top 3 performing creatives and rebuild 4–5 new variations: test user-generated content (client testimonials/transformations), before/afters, a "6-week reset" angle, and a pain-point opener ("tired of gyms full of…"). Pause anything running >3 weeks.

3. **Test a higher-ticket angle to protect margin.** Your profit per sale is ~£189 (£567÷3). If close rate holds at 75%, pushing the trial price up by £10–20 or bundling a nutrition plan might improve profit without hitting lead volume. Run a small test (£50/week) on a "premium trial" audience.

4. **Single biggest lever: Turn July's 75% close rate
